Multinational professional services network

**EY**,[[5]](#cite_note-rebranding-5)[[6]](#cite_note-rebranding2-6) previously known as **Ernst & Young**, is a British [multinational](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") [professional services](/wiki/Professional_services "Professional services") [network](/wiki/Partnership "Partnership") based in London, United Kingdom.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) Along with [Deloitte](/wiki/Deloitte "Deloitte"), [KPMG](/wiki/KPMG "KPMG") and [PwC](/wiki/PwC "PwC"), it is one of the [Big Four](/wiki/Big_Four_accounting_firms "Big Four accounting firms") professional services firms. The EY network is composed of member firms of **Ernst & Young Global Limited**, a UK company [limited by guarantee](/wiki/Company_limited_by_guarantee "Company limited by guarantee").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

EY is one of the largest [professional services networks](/wiki/Professional_services_network "Professional services network") in the world.[[8]](#cite_note-8) It primarily provides [assurance](/wiki/Assurance_services "Assurance services"), [tax](/wiki/Tax "Tax"), [information technology services](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") (including [managed services](/wiki/Managed_services "Managed services") in areas like [Cybersecurity](/wiki/Cybersecurity "Cybersecurity"), [Cloud](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing"), [Digital Transformation](/wiki/Digital_transformation "Digital transformation") and [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence")), [consulting](/wiki/Management_consulting "Management consulting"), and [advisory](/wiki/Corporate_services "Corporate services") services to its clients.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Ernst & Young Global Limited operates as a network of member firms structured as separate legal entities. As of mid-2024, it employs approximately 393,000 people across more than 700 offices in over 150 countries and territories.[[4]](#cite_note-headcount_2024-4) The firm's current partnership was formed in 1989 by a merger of two accounting firms: Ernst & Whinney and Arthur Young & Co.[[10]](#cite_note-10) It was named Ernst & Young until a rebranding campaign officially changed its name to EY in 2013,[[11]](#cite_note-11) although this initialism was already used informally prior to its sanctioning adoption.

In 2023, EY was the seventh-largest [privately owned organization](/wiki/Privately_held_company "Privately held company") in the United States,[[12]](#cite_note-12) and EY has for 25 years been continuously ranked on [*Fortune*](/wiki/Fortune_(magazine) "Fortune (magazine)") magazine's list of the [100 Best Companies to Work For](/wiki/100_Best_Companies_to_Work_For "100 Best Companies to Work For"), longer than any other accounting firm.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The firm has, however, repeatedly come under scrutiny for systemic issues in their training, hiring, and work culture.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history and mergers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history and mergers")]

EY resulted from several mergers of ancestor firms over the last century and a half, the oldest of which was founded in 1849, in England, as Harding & Pullein. That same year, this firm was joined by an accountant named Frederick Whinney, who, a decade later, became a partner. After his son joined the firm, it was later renamed Whinney, Smith & Whinney, in 1894.[[14]](#cite_note-history-14)

In 1903, the firm **Ernst & Ernst** was founded in Cleveland, Ohio, by [Alwin C. Ernst](/wiki/Alwin_C._Ernst "Alwin C. Ernst"), and his brother, Theodore Ernst. In 1906, **Arthur Young & Co.** was set up by a Scottish accountant, [Arthur Young](/wiki/Arthur_Young_(accountant) "Arthur Young (accountant)"), in Chicago. Starting in 1924, these two American firms became allied with prominent British firms; Young with Broads Paterson & Co.; and Ernst with the aforementioned Whinney Smith & Whinney. The latter of these two mergers spawned Anglo-American partnership **Ernst & Whinney** in 1979, then the fourth largest accountancy firm in the world.[[14]](#cite_note-history-14)

A decade later, in 1989, Ernst & Whinney merged with the fifth largest firm globally at the time, Arthur Young & Co., to create **Ernst & Young**.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Later developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Later developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Ernst%26young_logo.svg/250px-Ernst%26young_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ernst%26young_logo.svg)

Ernst & Young logo, 1990–2012

In October 1997, Ernst & Young announced plans to merge its global practices with professional services network [KPMG](/wiki/KPMG "KPMG")[[16]](#cite_note-16), to create the largest professional services organization in the world. The announcement came on the heels of an announced merger between [Price Waterhouse](/wiki/Price_Waterhouse "Price Waterhouse") and [Coopers & Lybrand](/wiki/Coopers_%26_Lybrand "Coopers & Lybrand") only a month earlier. These plans were soon abandoned in February 1998, due to several factors ranging from client opposition, antitrust issues, cost problems, and the anticipated difficulty of merging the two diverse firms and cultures.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The merger between Price Waterhouse and Coopers & Lybrand, however, went ahead as planned, creating [PwC](/wiki/PwC "PwC").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Ernst & Young expanded its consulting practice heavily during the 1980s and 1990s. During this time, the [U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission"), and various members of the investment community, began to raise concerns about a potential conflict of interests. This conflict would be brought about by firms offering both consulting and auditing services simultaneously to overlapping clients, a common practice among the "Big Five". In May 2000, Ernst & Young was the first of those firms to fully separate its consulting practices via a sale to the French IT services company [Capgemini](/wiki/Capgemini "Capgemini") for $11 billion, creating the new company Capgemini Ernst & Young, which was later renamed back to Capgemini.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Recent history, re-branding and expansion

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4b/Ernst_%26_Young_Sandton_Office.jpg/250px-Ernst_%26_Young_Sandton_Office.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ernst_%26_Young_Sandton_Office.jpg)

In 2002, Ernst & Young took on a large number of the clients previously working with [Arthur Andersen](/wiki/Arthur_Andersen "Arthur Andersen") following its collapse in connection with the [Enron scandal](/wiki/Enron_scandal "Enron scandal"), although it did not engage with any new Arthur Andersen clients from the United Kingdom, China, or the Netherlands.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Four years later, in 2006, Ernst & Young became the only member of the Big Four to have two member firms in the United States, with the inclusion of [Mitchell & Titus](/wiki/Mitchell_%26_Titus "Mitchell & Titus"), LLP, the largest minority-owned accounting firm in the United States.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Mitchell & Titus ended its membership in the EY network effective October 30, 2015.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In April 2009, [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters") reported that Ernst & Young, spurred by the global economic downturn, had launched a cost-saving initiative encouraging its staff in [China](/wiki/China "China") to take 40 days of low-pay leave between the summer of 2009 and the summer of 2010. Those who participated got a prorated salary equal to 20% of a regular salary, plus the benefits of a full-time employee. The initiative applied to employees in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") and [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), where the firm's employees numbered 8,500 in total.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In 2010, Ernst & Young acquired Terco, the Brazilian member firm of [Grant Thornton](/wiki/Grant_Thornton "Grant Thornton").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/EY_Logo_Beam_Tag_Stacked_RGB_EN.png/250px-EY_Logo_Beam_Tag_Stacked_RGB_EN.png)](/wiki/File:EY_Logo_Beam_Tag_Stacked_RGB_EN.png)

EY logo, 2013–2018

In 2013, the firm officially changed its brand from Ernst & Young to **EY**, and christened the accompanying tagline: "Building a better working world".[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[5]](#cite_note-rebranding-5)[[6]](#cite_note-rebranding2-6)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Also in 2013, the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") hired EY to help review [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") State's finances and help "verify and consult" the institution's administration, including the museums, post office and tax-free department store.[[28]](#cite_note-28) EY expanded further and acquired all of KPMG Denmark's operations including its 150 partners, 1,500 employees and 21 offices.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In 2014, EY acquired global strategy [consulting firm](/wiki/Consulting_firm "Consulting firm") [The Parthenon Group](/wiki/EY-Parthenon "EY-Parthenon"), gaining 350 consultants in its then-Transaction Advisory Services practice so that it could provide in-house strategy consulting services to its clients. The business unit has since been rebranded as [EY-Parthenon](/wiki/EY-Parthenon "EY-Parthenon") and is one of the most selective strategy consultancies worldwide.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 2015, EY opened its first global Security Operations Centre in [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram"), Kerala in India, and coincidentally invested $20 million over 5 years to combat the increasing threat of cybercrimes.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In 2017 EY announced it was opening an executive support center in Tucson, Arizona, US, creating 125 new jobs.[[32]](#cite_note-32) That same year, the company opened a Digital Security Operations Center, located in Muscat, Oman, to cover the EMEIA region as part of a $10 million investment.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 2018, EY opened a $4.4 million professional services center in Louisville, Kentucky, US, creating 125 new jobs,[[34]](#cite_note-34) and announced it would open an IT / tech hub in [Nashville, TN](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee"), US, creating 600 regional jobs.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In November 2022, it was announced EY had acquired the [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney")-headquartered data and analytics specialists, Bridge Business Consulting.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Project Everest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Project Everest")]

The *Wall Street Journal* reported in May 2022 that the firm might split its accounting and advisory divisions into two new, separate businesses.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The plan, referred to internally as "Project Everest" would involve the consulting business completing an [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering"), the proceeds of which would be used to compensate partners at the new, separate auditing company.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The firm's debt has proven to be an internal obstacle to the split.[[39]](#cite_note-wsj_ey2922-39) The debt is mostly owed to former partners of EY, taking the form of what the *Wall Street Journal* characterized as "effectively an unfunded pension plan".[[39]](#cite_note-wsj_ey2922-39) Would-be partners of the new accounting firm have expressed reservations as their descendant firm, the smaller of the new organizations, would presumably absorb most of the debt.[[39]](#cite_note-wsj_ey2922-39) On 5 September 2022, the firm announced that partners would vote on whether to split EY into two businesses.[[40]](#cite_note-40) EY's member firms in China, Hong Kong, Macau, and Israel stated that they would not split.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Rival firms such as KPMG and Deloitte have said they do not intend to imitate EY.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In March 2023, Julie Boland, head of EY US, stated in a webcast that the split would be temporarily paused amid internal debate over the proportioning of its tax service line among the proposed consulting and assurance spinoffs.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The firm cancelled Project Everest as the US portion of the firm withdrew its support for the split in April 2023.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Preparing and planning for the split cost EY $600 million.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In 2024, EY announced an alliance between [SAP](/wiki/SAP "SAP") Fioneer and EY ifb SE.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## Global structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Global structure")]

The firm is organized geographically into three areas: Europe, Middle East, India and Africa; the Americas; and Asia-Pacific.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In 2018, the company underwent a transformation of some of its region borders, primarily the union of its [CIS](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States") region (operating in the former Soviet Union) and the [CEE](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") region (Eastern Europe) to create the CESA block.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Services")]

Over the course of its operations, EY has transformed its business model and diversified its pool of offered services. Over the course of the last decade EY has substantially altered its business approach to offer a more comprehensive scope of services. This is mainly attributed to an intensified competition in the existing market of professional services, and competition in new markets: [investment banking](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking") and strategic consultancy. According to the latest published data, the company has the following four main service lines:[[50]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-50)

* [Assurance](/wiki/Assurance_services "Assurance services"): comprises Financial [Audit](/wiki/Audit "Audit"), [Financial Accounting](/wiki/Financial_Accounting "Financial Accounting") Advisory Services, CCaSS (Climate Change and Sustainability services) and Forensic & Integrity Services.
* [Tax](/wiki/Tax_advisor "Tax advisor"): [Transfer Pricing](/wiki/Transfer_pricing "Transfer pricing"), International Tax Services, Business Tax Compliance, Global Trade, [Indirect Tax](/wiki/Indirect_tax "Indirect tax"), Tax Accounting & Risk Advisory Services, Tax Technology and Transformation, [Transaction Tax](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions").
* [Consulting](/wiki/Management_consulting "Management consulting"): comprises three sub-service lines – Business Consulting, Technology Consulting, and People Advisory Services.
* [Strategy](/wiki/Strategy_consulting "Strategy consulting") and Transactions (SaT): deals with companies' capital transformation – including Valuation, Modelling, and Economics (VME); Transactions Due Diligence; Real Estate Advisory; M&A; Turnaround & [Restructuring](/wiki/Restructuring "Restructuring") (financial and operational); Corporate Finance.
* People Advisory Services: HR, immigration, workforce mobility programs
* Core Business Services: business development, marketing, legal, risk management

## Litigation and disputes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Litigation and disputes")]

### Audit practices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Audit practices")]

EY has been involved in many [accounting scandals](/wiki/Accounting_scandals "Accounting scandals"): [Bank of Credit and Commerce International](/wiki/Bank_of_Credit_and_Commerce_International "Bank of Credit and Commerce International") (1991), [Informix Corporation](/wiki/Informix_Corporation "Informix Corporation") (1996), [Sybase](/wiki/Sybase "Sybase") (1997), [Cendant](/wiki/Cendant "Cendant") (1998), [One.Tel](/wiki/One.Tel "One.Tel") (2001), [AOL](/wiki/AOL "AOL") (2002), [HealthSouth Corporation](/wiki/HealthSouth_Corporation "HealthSouth Corporation") (2003), [Chiquita Brands International](/wiki/Chiquita_Brands_International "Chiquita Brands International") (2004), [Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Lehman_Brothers "Lehman Brothers") (2010), [Sino-Forest Corporation](/wiki/Sino-Forest_Corporation "Sino-Forest Corporation") (2011), [Olympus Corporation](/wiki/Olympus_Corporation "Olympus Corporation") (2011), [Stagecoach Group](/wiki/Stagecoach_Group "Stagecoach Group") (2017),[[51]](#cite_note-shabong1-51) [Wirecard](/wiki/Wirecard_scandal "Wirecard scandal") (2020),[[52]](#cite_note-gu-calls-2020-52) [Luckin Coffee](/wiki/Luckin_Coffee "Luckin Coffee") (2020)[[53]](#cite_note-WSJ_Yang_20201016-53) and [NMC Health](/wiki/NMC_Health "NMC Health") (2020).[[54]](#cite_note-gu-nmc-54)

In 2004, Ernst & Young was criticized for forming a lucrative business arrangement with one of its audit clients, PeopleSoft, thus creating a conflict of interest. As a result, the firm was barred by the SEC from accepting any new publicly traded companies as audit clients for six months.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In April 2004, [Equitable Life](/wiki/The_Equitable_Life_Assurance_Society "The Equitable Life Assurance Society"), a UK life assurance company, sued EY after nearly collapsing but abandoned the case in September 2005. EY described the case as "a scandalous waste of time, money and resources for all concerned."[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In 2009, EY, the former auditors of [Sons of Gwalia](/wiki/Sons_of_Gwalia "Sons of Gwalia"), agreed to a $125m settlement over their role in the gold miner's collapse in 2004. [Ferrier Hodgson](/wiki/Ferrier_Hodgson "Ferrier Hodgson"), the company's administrator, had claimed EY was negligent over the accounting of gold and dollar hedging contracts. However, EY said that the proposed settlement was not an admission of any liability.[[57]](#cite_note-Ernst-57)

Following allegations by the [Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "Securities and Exchange Commission") (SEC) that EY had committed accounting fraud in its work auditing the books of [Bally Total Fitness](/wiki/Bally_Total_Fitness "Bally Total Fitness"), EY reached two settlements in 2008, including a fine of $8.5 million.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

EY Hong Kong resigned from the audit of Standard Water on when it emerged that although EY Hong Kong had signed off the audit, it had been effectively outsourced to the affiliate in mainland China, which had received 99.98% of the fee.[[59]](#cite_note-SCMP20130518-59) This was important because shareholders have less confidence in mainland auditors and because audit papers on the mainland are subject to state secrecy laws and can be withheld from outside regulators.[[59]](#cite_note-SCMP20130518-59) EY's quality and risk management leader (Greater China) even testified in the Court of First Instance that he was not sure whether there was a formal agreement covering the relationship between the two EY entities.[[59]](#cite_note-SCMP20130518-59) The court case in 2013 came as US regulators were taking an interest in similar cases of accounting fraud in mainland China.[[59]](#cite_note-SCMP20130518-59)

In September 2016, the US securities regulatory SEC fined EY US US$9.3 million for failures, including an auditor's romantic involvement with a client. Another partner on the team who was auditing a different public company became romantically involved with its chief accounting officer.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

In October 2016, EY settled with the SEC because they were unable to detect financial statement fraud that was committed by the [Weatherford](/wiki/Weatherford_International "Weatherford International") tax department.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Weatherford misstated their financial statements by manipulating the income tax line item in their financials. EY was Weatherford's independent auditors when the fraud was perpetrated.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In October 2016, [Mozilla](/wiki/Mozilla "Mozilla") stopped accepting [WebTrust](/wiki/WebTrust "WebTrust") audits from Ernst & Young Hong Kong[[63]](#cite_note-63) due to their failure "to detect multiple issues they should have detected" during their audits of [WoSign](/wiki/WoSign "WoSign").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

In February 2017, in response to questions regarding misissued certificates, [Symantec](/wiki/NortonLifeLock "NortonLifeLock") stated they would no longer accept WebTrust audits from E&Y Korea and E&Y Brazil due to deficiencies in these audits.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

According to *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")*, in 2019, EY had audited [WeWork](/wiki/WeWork "WeWork") the office-space company that "nearly collapsed after fumbling a planned initial public offering".[[53]](#cite_note-WSJ_Yang_20201016-53)

In April 2020, a former partner and whistleblower was awarded $10.8 million for ethical misconduct by EY in a Dubai gold audit by the high court in London. EY appealed the decision,[[66]](#cite_note-66) but then dropped the appeal in March 2021.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In 2020, EY failed to uncover $2 billion that was missing at [Wirecard](/wiki/Wirecard "Wirecard") AG, a German fintech payment processing company.[[53]](#cite_note-WSJ_Yang_20201016-53) This resulted in a lawsuit filed against EY in June 2020.[[68]](#cite_note-68) An investigation by the [Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag") revealed in April 2021 that EY's audits of defunct payments group Wirecard suffered from serious shortcomings over a period of years.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

EY also failed to identify $300 million in "fabricated sales" in their 2020 audit of the coffee chain [Luckin Coffee](/wiki/Luckin_Coffee "Luckin Coffee")[[53]](#cite_note-WSJ_Yang_20201016-53) and $5 billion in "undisclosed debt" at [NMC Health](/wiki/NMC_Health "NMC Health") and [Finablr](/wiki/Finablr "Finablr").[[53]](#cite_note-WSJ_Yang_20201016-53)

In August 2021, EY US agreed to pay US$10 million as part of a settlement with the SEC related to charges of auditor independence misconduct perpetrated by several of its partners to secure [Sealed Air](/wiki/Sealed_Air "Sealed Air") as a client.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

In August 2021, UK accounting regulatory [Financial Reporting Council](/wiki/Financial_Reporting_Council "Financial Reporting Council") (FRC) fined EY UK £3.5 million (US$4.8 million) for failing to challenge financial statements in its 2017 audit of UK transport company, [Stagecoach Group](/wiki/Stagecoach_Group "Stagecoach Group"). In addition, the auditing engagement partner Mark Harvey was sanctioned and fined £100,000. EY's fine was subsequently cut to £2.2 million for admitting to the failings, with Harvey's fine reduced to £70,000 for the same reason.[[51]](#cite_note-shabong1-51)

In December 2021, EY filed a criminal complaint against unknown persons with [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") prosecutors relating to the alleged leak of a classified German parliamentary report relating to its role in the collapse of payments firm, [Wirecard](/wiki/Wirecard "Wirecard"), to the German newspaper, [Handelsblatt](/wiki/Handelsblatt "Handelsblatt").[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In April 2022 the administrators of [NMC Health](/wiki/NMC_Health "NMC Health") filed a $2.5 billion lawsuit against EY, alleging negligence during its work on NMC's accounts spanning a seven-year period.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

In 2023, the [Public Company Accounting Oversight Board](/wiki/Public_Company_Accounting_Oversight_Board "Public Company Accounting Oversight Board") reported on four EY Canada audits conducted in 2022 and found that half had "multiple deficiencies".[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In April 2023, the German Abschlussprüferaufsichtsstelle (APAS) (the federal watchdog, responsible for the oversight on auditors) assessed, that EY has committed violations of duty on its Wirecard mandate and prohibited the company for two years to accept new audit mandates for companies at the German stock exchange.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

In December 2024, a lawsuit was filed against EY accusing it of assisting the UAE-based Brooge Petroleum and Gas Investment Company FZE in defrauding investors during a 2019 [special-purpose acquisition company](/wiki/Special-purpose_acquisition_company "Special-purpose acquisition company") (SPAC) merger. It alleges that Brooge fabricated financial data through fake invoices, customers and affiliated parties and forged 30-80% of its revenues between 2018 and 2020. EY allegedly supported Brooge’s scheme by “issuing unqualified audit opinions”. The lawsuit was filed by a group of Boorge Energy shareholders, who accused EY of fraud and violations of the [Securities Exchange Act of 1934](/wiki/Securities_Exchange_Act_of_1934 "Securities Exchange Act of 1934"). The plaintiffs were seeking damages, interest, and legal fees.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

In April 2025, the company was fined £4.9 million by the UK [Financial Reporting Council](/wiki/Financial_Reporting_Council "Financial Reporting Council") for 'serious breaches in standards' in its audits of [Thomas Cook](/wiki/Thomas_Cook_Group "Thomas Cook Group"), which collapsed in 2019.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

### Exam cheating by audit professionals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Exam cheating by audit professionals")]

In June 2022, the SEC fined the firm US$100 million "for cheating by its audit professionals on exams required to obtain and maintain [Certified Public Accountant](/wiki/Certified_Public_Accountant "Certified Public Accountant") (CPA) licenses, and for withholding evidence of this misconduct from the SEC's Enforcement Division during the Division's investigation of the matter".[[78]](#cite_note-sec-2022-78) EY admitted "the facts underlying the SEC’s charges"[[78]](#cite_note-sec-2022-78) and the penalty is a record imposed on a US audit firm.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The [Canadian Public Accountability Board](/wiki/Canadian_Public_Accountability_Board "Canadian Public Accountability Board") has announced that it will investigate whether EY's Canadian arm was involved in similar practices.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

### Investment banking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Investment banking")]

In 2009, in the [Anglo Irish Bank hidden loans controversy](/wiki/Anglo_Irish_Bank_hidden_loans_controversy "Anglo Irish Bank hidden loans controversy"), EY was criticised by politicians[[81]](#cite_note-81) and the shareholders of Anglo Irish Bank for failing to detect large loans to [Seán FitzPatrick](/wiki/Se%C3%A1n_FitzPatrick "Seán FitzPatrick"), its chairman, during its audits. The Irish Government had to subsequently take full ownership of the Bank at a cost of €28 billion.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) The Irish Chartered Accountants Regulatory Board appointed John Purcell to investigate.[[84]](#cite_note-84) EY said it "fundamentally disagrees with the decision to initiate a formal disciplinary process" and that "there has been no adverse finding made against EY in respect of the audit of Anglo Irish Bank."[[85]](#cite_note-85)

In 2009, EY agreed to pay US$200m out of court to settle a [negligence](/wiki/Negligence "Negligence") claim by the liquidators of [Akai](/wiki/Akai "Akai") Holdings.[[86]](#cite_note-SCMP20100127-86) Separately the firm was accused of falsifying and doctoring documents it presented to defend against the negligence claim by Akai's liquidators.[[87]](#cite_note-SCMP20090930-87) In a separate lawsuit, a former EY senior partner from 1984 to 1991, Cristopher Ho, and his listed company, Grande Holdings, paid over US$100m to Akai creditors to settle Akai's liquidators' claim that Ho conspired with Ting of stripping assets from Akai.[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) Police raided the Hong Kong office and arrested an EY partner who had been an audit manager on the Akai account from December 1997, although audit documents had been doctored dating back to 1994.[[87]](#cite_note-SCMP20090930-87) Akai was said to be the firm's largest client for most of the 1990s from Hong Kong.[[90]](#cite_note-90)
The EY partner for the Akai account between 1991 and 1999, [David Sun Tak-kei](/wiki/David_Sun_Tak-kei "David Sun Tak-kei"), faced no charges and went on to become co-managing partner for EY China.[[87]](#cite_note-SCMP20090930-87) A few months later EY settled a similar claim of up to HK$300m from the liquidators of Moulin Global Eyecare, an audit client of the Hong Kong affiliate between 2002 and 2004.[[86]](#cite_note-SCMP20100127-86) The liquidators described the Moulin accounts as a "morass of [dodginess](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/dodginess "wikt:dodginess")".[[86]](#cite_note-SCMP20100127-86)

The [Valukas Report](/wiki/Valukas_Report "Valukas Report") issued in 2010[[91]](#cite_note-91) charged that [Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Lehman_Brothers "Lehman Brothers") engaged in a practice known as [repo 105](/wiki/Repo_105 "Repo 105") and that EY, Lehman's auditor, was aware of it. EY was accused of professional [malpractice](/wiki/Malpractice "Malpractice") regarding the lack of disclosure of Lehman's repo 105 practice in Lehman's public filings.[[92]](#cite_note-Fraternite-92) New York prosecutors announced in 2010[[93]](#cite_note-93) that they have sued the firm. David Goldfarb, a Lehman CFO who concocted the repo 105 balance sheet window dressing technique was a former senior partner of EY.[[92]](#cite_note-Fraternite-92) EY said that its last audit of Lehman Brothers was for the fiscal year ending 30 November 2007 and that Lehman's financial statements were fairly presented in accordance with [Generally Accepted Accounting Principles](/wiki/Generally_Accepted_Accounting_Principles "Generally Accepted Accounting Principles").[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) In March 2015, EY settled Lehman-related lawsuits with municipalities in New Jersey and California.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

In 2014, tax arrangements negotiated by EY for [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company"), [Koch Industries](/wiki/Koch_Industries "Koch Industries"), [Skype](/wiki/Skype "Skype"), and other [multinational corporations](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") became public in the so-called *[Luxembourg Leaks](/wiki/Luxembourg_Leaks "Luxembourg Leaks")*. The disclosure of these and other tax arrangements led to controversial discussions about [tax avoidance](/wiki/Tax_avoidance "Tax avoidance").[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)

In 2015, EY's member firm in Japan, Ernst & Young ShinNihon, was fined ¥2.1 billion (US$17.4 million) for failing to spot irregularities since 2008 while auditing its client [Toshiba](/wiki/Toshiba "Toshiba"), which was Japan's worst accounting scandal in years. The firm was also suspended from taking up new business for three months. An official from Japan's [Financial Services Agency](/wiki/Financial_Services_Agency "Financial Services Agency") (FSA) described that "there was a grave breach of duty". The firm's CEO and chairman, Koichi Hanabusa stepped down the following month to take responsibility and monthly salaries for 19 employees were cut from 20 per cent to 50 per cent.[[101]](#cite_note-ReutersToshiba-101)[[102]](#cite_note-FTShinNihon-102) In an unusual move, the FSA publicly named seven accountants involved in the audit who were accused of failing to exercise due caution and signing off on false financial documents.[[102]](#cite_note-FTShinNihon-102) The FSA also said the "firm’s operations were deeply improper".[[102]](#cite_note-FTShinNihon-102) ShinNihon, at the time, was Japan's biggest accounting firm, with 3,500 certified accountants and 4,000 clients.[[101]](#cite_note-ReutersToshiba-101) ShinNihon audited about 960 listed companies in Japan, the most among the [Big Four](/wiki/Big_Four_accounting_firms "Big Four accounting firms"), as reported in 2015.[[103]](#cite_note-NikkeiEYSN-103) ShinNihon had audited Toshiba for over 60 years and the firm had around 70 staff serving Toshiba before the accounting scandal broke.[[103]](#cite_note-NikkeiEYSN-103)

In 2017, Ernst & Young Baltic, member of the EY network, was accused of using the emission assumptions of highly polluting EURO II trucks (manufactured before 2001) to falsely increase the socio-economic benefits of the new railway for the period 2026–2055 by 3 billion euros in the [Rail Baltica](/wiki/Rail_Baltica "Rail Baltica") Cost-Benefit Analysis. The total mistakes amounted to more than 4 billion euros and constituted 20% of the total socio-economic benefit of the Rail Baltica.[[104]](#cite_note-104) Correction of the mistakes made the project unfeasible. EY has refused to provide any comments to the media regarding the public accusations.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

## Employment culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Employment culture")]

### Sexist training program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sexist training program")]

In October 2019, *[HuffPost](/wiki/HuffPost "HuffPost")* broke a story about a New Jersey "Power-Presence-Purpose" training seminar purportedly to empower female employees, but which was, as characterized by *HuffPost*, "full of out of touch advice".[[106]](#cite_note-106) Women were told to concentrate on their appearance, not to show too much skin, and not to speak too much. One participant said it was basically a "women bashing" exercise. "You have to offer your thoughts in a benign way...You have to be the perfect [Stepford wife](/wiki/Stepford_wife "Stepford wife")... It felt like they were being turned into someone who is super-smiley, who never confronts anyone" she said.[[107]](#cite_note-107) In 2021, EY agreed to pay the state of New Jersey $100,000 and set up a $500,000 scholarship following its investigation of the program to resolve claims EY broke state law against discrimination.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

### Working rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Working rights")]

In April 2021, EY's second-year auditor staff in their Barcelona office sent an email to their line managers to complain about the long hours that they had to work, which were sometimes reaching 84 working hours per week.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

### Elizabeth Broderick & Co. Culture Review

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Elizabeth Broderick & Co. Culture Review")]

In July 2023, an independent culture review by the former Australian sex discrimination commissioner [Elizabeth Broderick](/wiki/Elizabeth_Broderick "Elizabeth Broderick") was released after being commissioned by EY following the suicide of an auditor in the Sydney office in August 2022. Over 4,500 participants from the Australian and New Zealand EY offices participated through online surveys, interviews, written submissions, and group listening sessions. The report revealed that 15% of EY employees have experienced instances of either bullying, sexual harassment, or racism. The report also detailed the long working hours, with approximately two in five employees considering quitting due to long work hours and 46% of respondents reporting that their health has been negatively affected due to the long hours. The culture review revealed that over 31% of EY employees worked over 51 hours per week routinely, and 11% of EY employees working over 61 hours per week routinely.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

In April 2024, EY apologized for its workplace culture and said it has started a "time-owed-in-lieu pilot" and is "making strong progress" on other report recommendations.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

### Toxic work culture in India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Toxic work culture in India")]

Anna Sebastian Perayil, a 26-year-old [chartered accountant](/wiki/Chartered_accountant_(India) "Chartered accountant (India)"), died in July 2024, four months after starting work at the Pune, India, EY office. Her family alleged that her death was due to stress caused by overwork, [presenteeism](/wiki/Presenteeism "Presenteeism") and exhaustion. Her death led to nationwide outrage and debate on [toxic work culture](/wiki/Toxic_workplace "Toxic workplace") in India.[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)

[Shobha Karandlaje](/wiki/Shobha_Karandlaje "Shobha Karandlaje"), the Minister of State for the [Ministry of Labour and Employment](/wiki/Ministry_of_Labour_and_Employment_(India) "Ministry of Labour and Employment (India)"), said in a post on [X](/wiki/X_Twitter "X Twitter") that her ministry has officially taken up the complaint. Other political leaders like the Maharashtra Deputy Chief Minister [Ajit Pawar](/wiki/Ajit_Pawar "Ajit Pawar") and [Bharatiya Janata Party](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party") leader [Rajeev Chandrasekhar](/wiki/Rajeev_Chandrasekhar "Rajeev Chandrasekhar"), have expressed sadness and support for a probe.[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)

## Sponsorships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Sponsorships")]

Ernst & Young is involved in many sponsorships, including its *[World Entrepreneur of the Year Award](/wiki/Ernst_%26_Young_Entrepreneur_of_the_Year_Award "Ernst & Young Entrepreneur of the Year Award")* program, held in over 60 countries.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

The awards are an annual award program sponsored by Ernst & Young in recognition of [entrepreneurship](/wiki/Entrepreneurship "Entrepreneurship").
Founded in 1986 in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") as a single award, the program now runs in all 50 U.S. states and in more than 60 countries.[[117]](#cite_note-EYeoty-117)

At the country and territory levels, programs begin with nomination[[118]](#cite_note-118) of entrepreneurs who demonstrate specific criteria. The award may be given to multiple individuals per year. For example, in the U.S. and other countries, there are multiple regional and category winners, spanning fields such as for retail and consumer products, technology, family business, energy, chemical and mining, food products and services, real estate, hospitality, and construction, financial services, digital media, and transformational ventures.[[119]](#cite_note-119) However, in each country, only one company and its leader(s) are recognized as the overall award recipient.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Since 1986, over 10,000 founders, CEOs, and business leaders have received awards.[[121]](#cite_note-hof-121)

### Entrepreneurial Winning Women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Entrepreneurial Winning Women")]

Entrepreneurial Winning Women is a global EY initiative that identifies women founders and supports them to help them scale. Launched in 2008, the program has expanded to 55 countries and graduated over 1,000 entrepreneurs.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

In North America and Canada, eligibility focuses on founding CEOs who retain substantial ownership and lead privately held businesses.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

Notable alumnae include [Sheri Jacobson](/wiki/Sheri_Jacobson "Sheri Jacobson") (UK & Ireland, class of 2018), [Brianne West](/wiki/Brianne_West "Brianne West") (Asia–Pacific, class of 2020), [Paula Fray](/wiki/Paula_Fray "Paula Fray") (Africa, class of 2015), [Jamie Kern Lima](/wiki/Jamie_Kern_Lima "Jamie Kern Lima") (North America, class of 2013)[[124]](#cite_note-124) and [Amy Buckner Chowdhry](/wiki/Amy_Buckner_Chowdhry "Amy Buckner Chowdhry") (North America, class of 2011).[[125]](#cite_note-125)

### Art and media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Art and media")]

EY UK sponsors exhibitions of works by famous artists, such as [Cézanne](/wiki/C%C3%A9zanne "Cézanne"), [Picasso](/wiki/Picasso "Picasso"), [Bonnard](/wiki/Pierre_Bonnard "Pierre Bonnard"), [Monet](/wiki/Monet "Monet"), [Rodin](/wiki/Auguste_Rodin "Auguste Rodin") and [Renoir](/wiki/Renoir "Renoir"). The most recent of these was *Maharaja: the Splendour of India's Royal Courts* at the [Victoria and Albert Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum").[[126]](#cite_note-126)

In addition, EY sponsors the educational children's show *[Cyberchase](/wiki/Cyberchase "Cyberchase")* on [PBS Kids](/wiki/PBS_Kids "PBS Kids") under the [PBS Kids GO!](/wiki/PBS_Kids_GO! "PBS Kids GO!") television brand, in an effort to improve [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") literacy in children.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

EY in the UK sponsors the [ITEM club](/wiki/ITEM_club "ITEM club").[[128]](#cite_note-128)

EY in the UK has set up the [National Equality Standard](/wiki/National_Equality_Standard "National Equality Standard") (NES), an initiative developed for business which sets clear equality, diversity and inclusion (EDI) criteria against which companies are assessed. The [National Equality Standard](/wiki/National_Equality_Standard "National Equality Standard") (NES) is currently the only industry recognized national standard for EDI in the UK.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

EY in the UK has set up *EY Foundation*, a new UK charity set up to support young people and entrepreneurs.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ernst_%26_Young&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sports")]

On 8 September 2011, [Rio 2016](/wiki/Rio_2016 "Rio 2016") made the announcement that EY would be an official sponsor of the *[2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics")* to be held in Brazil, as the exclusive provider of professional services – consulting and auditing – for Rio 2016 organizing committee.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

EY was an Official Partner to the [2012](/wiki/2012_Ryder_Cup "2012 Ryder Cup") and the [2014 Ryder Cups](/wiki/2014_Ryder_Cup "2014 Ryder Cup"),[[132]](#cite_note-132) it partners with the [British and Irish Lions](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Lions "British and Irish Lions").,[[133]](#cite_note-133) and it has a longstanding relationship with the [2011 Tour de France](/wiki/2011_Tour_de_France "2011 Tour de France") winner [Cadel Evans](/wiki/Cadel_Evans "Cadel Evans").[[134]](#cite_note-134)

In April 2019, EY announced a two-year-long collaboration with [USA Rugby](/wiki/USA_Rugby "USA Rugby"), where EY would act as their Official Principal Partner. The partnership was focusing on digital and technological transformations, as well as innovating and growing the game in the US, while specifically mentioning an overhaul of USA Rugby's diversity and inclusiveness program.[[135]](#cite_note-135)
